data.title: U.S. HVAC shipments show mixed trends in February, water heaters decline
data.description:
U.S. shipments of residential and commercial water heaters saw notable year-over-year declines in February 2025, while gas furnace shipments surged, according to data released by the Air-Conditioning, Heating and Refrigeration Institute.

Water heater shipments decline

Residential gas storage water heater shipments dropped 5.3% to 376,403 units, down from 397,529 units in February 2024. Shipments of residential electric storage models also declined, falling 6.4% to 425,332 units compared with 454,577 units a year earlier.

 

 

In the commercial segment, gas storage water heater shipments decreased 5.7% to 7,660 units from 8,120 the previous year. Commercial electric storage models fell 3.9%, totaling 13,409 units versus 13,950 in February 2024.

Furnace shipments surge

In contrast, shipments of warm air furnaces posted strong gains. Gas furnace shipments jumped 20.7% year-over-year to 259,405 units, up from 214,961. Oil furnace shipments also rose significantly, increasing 20.4% to 2,931 units from 2,434 the previous year.

Cooling equipment shows mixed performance

Total U.S. shipments of central air conditioners and air-source heat pumps declined 3.2% to 636,302 units in February 2025, down from 657,509 units a year earlier.

Air conditioner shipments were the main contributor to the decline, falling 8.1% to 342,542 units. However, air-source heat pump shipments grew 3.2% to 293,760 units, up from 284,612 in February 2024.

 

 

The latest figures reflect a complex demand landscape influenced by shifting weather patterns, economic factors and evolving energy efficiency preferences across residential and commercial markets.

data.title: Around 10% of world is using OpenAI's systems: Sam Altman
data.description:
OpenAI chief executive Sam Altman said on Friday the generative AI startup had reached about 800 million users. 

"Something like 10% of the world uses our systems, now a lot," Altman said to host Chris Anderson at the TED 2025 event, referring to broadly hundreds of millions of users. 

Anderson revealed that Altman had said the startup's users were growing rapidly, doubling in a "just a few weeks." 

The Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT)-backed startup's growth has received a boost from viral features, such as the recently launched ability to create images and videos in various styles, including the style of legendary Japanese animation studio, Studio Ghibli. 

On March 31, Altman said in a post on X that the company had hit one million users in five days, as users flocked to ChatGPT to try the viral Ghibli photo trend. He added that OpenAI had added a million users in one hour. 

When asked about if the company had considered compensating artists for creating works in their style, Altman said there could possibly be prompts that could trigger payments for specific artists. 

"I think it would be cool to figure out a new model where if you say 'I want to do it in the name of this artist' and they opt in, there’s a revenue model there," said Altman. 

Altman added the company had guardrails in place to prevent the AI model from generating images in the styles of specific artists or creators. 

He also spoke about the company's work on AI agents, models that can operate autonomously on behalf of users. 

OpenAI is now the world's most valuable startup, overtaking TikTok-owner ByteDance (BDNCE) with a valuation of $300 billion. Its valuation has nearly doubled since October 2024.

data.title: Notable healthcare headlines of the week: Norvatis, Novo Nordisk, Eli Lilly in focus
data.description:
Wall Street on Friday overcame a volatile morning trading session to push higher, ending a wild week on a positive note. Tariffs dominated headlines, with the latest being that the Trump administration had made "very good progress" on trade negotiations. 

The S&P 500 Health Care Index Sector (NYSEARCA:XLV) was marginally up (+0.31%) during the week.

The top S&P 500 healthcare gainers and losers for the last week are as follows:

Top Gainers:

  • Humana (HUM) +15.99%
  • UnitedHealth (UNH) +14.17%
  • DexCom (DXCM) +10.66%
  • Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) +9.51
  • CVS Health (CVS) +9.48

· Top Losers:

  • Charles River Laboratories International (CRL) -27.14%
  • Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY) -8.30%
  • Biogen (BIIB) -6.42%
  • AbbVie (ABBV) -6.37%
  • Incyte (INCY) -6.12%

Here are some of the important healthcare stories from this week:

Novartis is eyeing $23B investment in U.S.

Novartis (NVS) announced Thursday it plans to invest $23B to build and expand 10 U.S. plants to ensure that all key medicines developed by the company are manufactured domestically. The announcement comes amid President Donald Trump’s repeated threats to impose tariffs on pharmaceutical goods. The expansion is anticipated to generate over 1,000 jobs at Novartis (NVS) and roughly 4,000 additional jobs. 

J&J upgraded Pfizer, AbbVie downgraded as Goldman reviews U.S. biopharma coverage

Goldman Sachs made significant rating changes across its U.S. biopharma coverage on Tuesday, upgrading the likes of Eli Lilly (LLY) and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) while downgrading Bristol Myers (BMY), Pfizer (PEE), and AbbVie (ABBV).

Goldman Sachs analyst Asad Haider upgraded Eli Lilly (LLY) to Buy from Neutral, while decreasing its price target to $888 from $892 per share.

The brokerage firm upgraded Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) to Buy from Neutral, increasing price target to $172 from $157.

The analyst downgraded AbbVie (ABBV) to Neutral from Buy and trimmed its 12-month price target to $194 from $212 per share.

Goldman downgraded Pfizer (PEF) to Neutral from Buy and lowered its 12-month price target to $25 from $32. 

The firm also downgraded Bristol Myers (BMY) to Neutral from Buy and trimmed its 12-month price target to $55 from $67 

Novo reportedly eyeing $1.09B investment in Brazil to expand obesity drug production

Novo Nordisk (NVO) plans to invest 6.4B reais ($1.09B) in Brazil to boost the production of its highly popular diabetes/weight loss drugs Ozempic and Wegovy, as well as other injectables targeted at chronic diseases, Reuters reported Monday.

Eli Lilly to recognize $1.57B in Q1 as acquired R&D charges

Eli Lilly (LLY) announced that its financial results for Q1 2025, prepared under the U.S. generally accepted accounting principles (GAAP), will include $1.57B recognized on a pre-tax basis as acquired In-Process Research and Development (IPR&D) charges.

The acquired IPR&D charges are expected to impact the Indiana-based pharma giant’s earnings for the quarter, creating a headwind of about $1.57 per share for both GAAP and non-GAAP earnings, LLY said in a statement on Thursday.

According to Wall Street estimates, Eli Lilly (LLY) is set to report $4.70 per share of earnings on average on revenue of $12.8B for Q1 2025.

Bristol-Myers gets Pomalyst antitrust case dismissed

A federal judge in New York has dismissed a proposed class action filed by health insurer Blue Cross Blue Shield of Louisiana alleging that Bristol Myers Squibb (BMY) ran an illegal scheme to protect its monopoly over its blockbuster multiple myeloma treatment Pomalyst.

U.S. District Judge Edgardo Ramos in Manhattan dismissed the case, noting that the plaintiffs had failed to establish that the company had violated the federal Sherman antitrust law, Reuters reported, citing a 70-page court document made public on Tuesday.

Novavax in selloff after RFK Jr.’s comments on COVID vaccine

Novavax (NVAX) shares sold off on Thursday after Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. cast doubts about the efficacy of the company's COVID shot amid a delay for its U.S. approval. During an interview with Dr. Jon Lapook of CBS News, RFK Jr. attributed the delay to the vaccine’s single-antigen composition, which he said has never worked against respiratory diseases.

data.title: Joby Aviation downgraded at Morgan Stanley amid tariff risks to aerospace
data.description:
Joby Aviation (NYSE:JOBY) on Friday was downgraded to Equal-weight from a previous investment rating of Overweight by analysts at Morgan Stanley because of heightened macroeconomic volatility.

The broader aerospace sector faces growing risks from global tariffs, with supply chain fragility compounded by Boeing’s (NYSE:BA) production challenges and lingering effects of the COVID-19 pandemic, said Kristine Liwag, research analyst at the financial-services firm. 

Tariffs will likely dominate the conversation heading into first-quarter earnings and are prompting a shift in investor preference toward more defensive aftermarket aerospace companies, she warned in an April 11 report.

“The uncertainty regarding tariffs increases the risk of further disruption of an already vulnerable supply chain,” Liwag wrote.

She emphasized that complex global supply chains in the aerospace sector are particularly exposed to macro shocks. As a result, Morgan Stanley reiterated its preference for aftermarket-focused companies, citing their stronger pricing power and flexibility in passing through costs.

Among the firms Liwag favors are Overweight-rated Transdigm (NYSE:TDG), RBC Bearings (NYSE:RBC), and Howmet Aerospace (NYSE:HWM), the latter known for its historical use of pass-through clauses. Other preferred names include Loar Holdings (NYSE:LOAR) and Equal-weight-rated Heico (NYSE:HEI).

Demand destruction and resilience

While investors are concerned about how much demand destruction the aerospace sector can withstand, Liwag argued the industry remains structurally undersupplied. She pointed to the aftermath of Covid-19, which triggered the harshest demand shock in aviation history — air traffic plummeted 95% in April 2020 — but noted that current disruptions are unlikely to reach the same scale.

“New aircraft production from Boeing has been limited for the past six years,” Liwag noted, adding that 2025 deliveries are expected to reach just 69% of 2018 levels.

Engine reliability issues have also constrained the utility of newly delivered aircraft, suggesting that existing demand buffers could help the sector weather near-term turbulence.

Still, tariff-related delays are already surfacing. Delta Air Lines Chief Executive Ed Bastian recently stated that the airline would defer aircraft deliveries subject to tariffs, vowing not to absorb those costs.

Similarly, Howmet Aerospace (NYSE:HWM) has declared a “force majeure” event on certain components impacted by tariffs—an early sign that supply constraints may intensify.

Robust backlogs cushion OEMs

Despite mounting headwinds, Liwag believes Boeing’s (NYSE:BA) aircraft backlog provides a cushion against near-term disruptions. She noted that even if the company faced up to 1,500 cancellations, its 737 production slots would likely remain sold out through 2030. 

Boeing’s (BA) first-quarter deliveries for the 737 program also beat expectations, with 105 aircraft delivered versus Morgan Stanley’s forecast of 84.

Morgan Stanley also adjusted several price targets across the aerospace sector, citing macro and tariff-related risks. Hexcel (NYSE:HXL) was lowered to $50 from $61, Textron (NYSE:TXT) to $71 from $82, AerCap (NYSE:AER) to $101 from $112, Joby (NYSE:JOBY) to $7 from $10, CAE to C$36 from C$37 and Fortress Transportation and Infrastructure Investors (NASDAQ:FTAI) to $138 from $168.

Ultimately, Liwag concluded that firms with strong pricing power, proprietary products and technological advantages are best positioned to navigate the current landscape.

“The most successful mitigants to tariffs are pricing power and pass-through clauses,” she emphasized, reinforcing the firm’s conviction in aftermarket-exposed stocks over original equipment manufacturers.

data.title: To tariff or not to tariff: Trump to provide update on chips tariffs on Monday
data.description:
President Donald Trump said on Saturday he will provide an update on the administration's proposed semiconductor tariffs on Monday. 

"I'll give you that answer on Monday," Trump told reporters aboard Air Force One.

Yesterday, the Trump administration excluded smartphones, computers and other popular consumer electronics from reciprocal tariffs. The exclusions include computer semiconductors and equipment used to manufacture semiconductors. 

Trump has previously suggested sectoral tariffs for semiconductors, similar to the ones applied on steel and aluminum. "The chips are starting very soon," the President said earlier in the month. 

In February, Trump suggested tariffs in the "in the neighborhood of 25%" on autos and similar duties on semiconductors and pharmaceutical imports.

data.title: Goldman Sachs warns of market volatility, low liquidity as S&P 500 nears target
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SP500) is hovering close to Goldman Sachs’ three-month target of 5,300, but near-term risks appear skewed to the downside, said David Kostin, the firm’s chief U.S. equity strategist. In an April 11 report to clients, he pointed to mounting recession risks, heightened volatility and deteriorating market liquidity as key factors shaping the current environment.

“While light investor positioning could create room for further gains if economic or policy outlooks improve, markets are not fully pricing in the elevated risk of a recession,” Kostin cautioned.

He added that realized volatility on the S&P 500 (SP500) has surged to levels last seen during the Covid-19 crisis, with the VIX (VIX) briefly spiking to 52 earlier in the week.

Over the past two weeks, the S&P 500 (SP500) has seen dramatic swings — including one of its worst single-day declines in decades with a 6% drop last Friday, followed by a 10% rally after President Trump announced a 90-day pause on newly introduced tariffs. That rally marked the index’s third-best daily gain since 1950.

Deteriorating liquidity

Kostin highlighted a deterioration in equity market liquidity, noting it has created a negative feedback loop with volatility.

“Sudden liquidity shocks are typically associated with a wider range of returns,” he said.

Goldman’s S&P 500 Liquidity Tracker — which monitors bid-ask spreads, futures market depth and the price impact of trading volume — shows that liquidity has sharply declined in the past month, though it remains above pandemic-era lows.

One clear sign of market stress came on April 7, when the bid-ask spread for the median S&P 500 (SP500) stock widened to 22 basis points — the highest since March 2020. Similarly, the depth of futures markets fell into the 3rd percentile of the past two decades, underscoring the difficulty in executing large trades without moving prices.

Surprisingly stable performance

Despite these conditions, the relative performance between high- and low-liquidity stocks has remained unusually stable. Goldman’s High Liquidity basket outperformed its Low Liquidity counterpart by 500 basis points earlier in the year, but the trend has flattened during recent drawdowns.

Typically, high-liquidity stocks underperform during market selloffs because they’re easier to offload, but Kostin noted that “quality” characteristics — such as stronger balance sheets and more stable earnings — have helped buoy performance.

Goldman also rebalanced its liquidity baskets, which consist of 50 equal-weighted Russell 1000 stocks with the highest and lowest liquidity ratios. High-liquidity names generally offer narrower bid-ask spreads and higher trading volume, while low-liquidity stocks tend to be more thinly traded and volatile.

While the S&P 500 remains near its target, the current environment — marked by extreme volatility, policy uncertainty, and fragile liquidity — warrants caution, Kostin said.

data.title: Japan is said to rule out using Treasuries as leverage in tariff talks
data.description:
Japan has no intention of leveraging its holdings of U.S. government debt as a bargaining chip in upcoming trade negotiations with Washington, Bloomberg News reported on Sunday.

Itsunori Onodera, the policy chief of Japan’s ruling Liberal Democratic Party, emphasized that as a key U.S. ally, Japan would avoid any actions that could disrupt financial markets.

“It’s not in our interest to take steps that would destabilize U.S. Treasury markets,” he was quoted as saying on national broadcaster NHK.

His remarks come amid heightened market sensitivity. U.S. Treasury yields jumped sharply last week, marking their biggest surge since the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic, as investors speculated over possible shifts in foreign demand for U.S. debt. Concerns have grown that major reserve holders, including China, might be reassessing their exposure due to the Trump administration’s aggressive trade posture.

Japan is currently seeking an exemption from the sweeping U.S. tariffs that took effect on April 9. Meanwhile, Washington is pressing Tokyo for greater market access for American agricultural exports and liquefied natural gas. The tariffs have hit Japan with a 24% rate, and its vital auto sector now faces a 25% duty.

Onodera also called for Japan to raise the tariff issue at the World Trade Organization and expressed solidarity with neighboring countries in Asia, many of which have been subjected to even steeper U.S. tariffs. He added that Japan aims to deepen regional coordination through the Association of Southeast Asian Nations, Bloomberg News reported.

Dear Readers: We recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Earnings week ahead: BAC, GS, JNJ, TSM, UNH, ABT, UAL, C, NFLX and more
data.description:
Earnings season picks up pace in the upcoming holiday-shortened week with a lineup of heavyweights across banking, healthcare, tech, and industrials. 

Key names set to report include Bank of America (BAC), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Netflix (NASDAQ:NFLX), Taiwan Semiconductor (NYSE:TSM), and Citigroup (NYSE:C). 

Other closely watched updates will come from ASML (ASML), Kinder Morgan (NYSE:KMI), UnitedHealth (UNH), Goldman Sachs (NYSE:GS), Blackstone (BX), Abbott Labs (NYSE:ABT), American Express (AXP), United Airlines (UAL), Alcoa (AA), U.S. Bancorp (USB), and Prologis (PLD), offering a broad look at macro and sector-specific trends heading into the heart of the season.

Below is a rundown of major quarterly updates anticipated in the week of April 14 to April 18:

Monday, April 14

Following a series of bank earnings reported at the end of last week, Goldman Sachs (NYSE:GS) is scheduled to post its Q1 results before Monday’s opening bell. Analysts project a 6% Y/Y EPS increase and a 4% rise in revenue.

While Wall Street maintains a Buy rating on the stock, Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating system holds a more cautious Hold due to concerns around valuation and profitability.

Last month, Oppenheimer downgraded the stock to Perform from Outperform, citing a stalled M&A rebound amid tariff uncertainty and broader macro risks.

Similarly, SA contributor Daniel Urbina recently downgraded Goldman Sachs (NYSE:GS) to Sell from Buy, citing a shift in the macroeconomic outlook since February. He noted that recession concerns and weaker economic growth could weigh on IPOs, M&A activity, and fee income. While valuations remain near historical averages, Urbina argues that the stock doesn’t reflect the risk of a sharper slowdown, making the current pricing less compelling.

  • Consensus EPS Estimates: $12.28
  • Consensus Revenue Estimates: $14.71B
  • Earnings Insight: Goldman Sachs has beaten revenue expectations in 7 of the past 8 quarters, missing EPS estimates twice in that span.

Also reporting: M&T Bank (MTB), Ontrak (OTRK), Pinnacle Financial Partners (PNFP), and more.

Tuesday, April 15

Bank of America (BAC)

Bank of America (BAC) is set to report its Q1 earnings before Tuesday’s market open, alongside Citigroup (NYSE:C), with analysts expecting modest single-digit year-over-year growth in both earnings and revenue. The results will follow a wave of Q1 reports from peers, including upbeat numbers from JPMorgan (JPM) and Morgan Stanley (MS), and mixed outcomes from BlackRock (BLK) and Wells Fargo (WFC), a lineup that has broadly improved sentiment across the banking sector.

The bank gets a Hold rating from Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating system, in contrast to the Strong Buy consensus among Wall Street analysts.

SA contributor Julia Ostian maintains a Hold rating on Bank of America, citing heightened economic uncertainty and a shift in market sentiment heading into the Q1 2025 release. While BAC delivered a solid Q4 2024 performance, the stock has notably lagged peers like JPMorgan and Wells Fargo this year, raising questions about its resilience. Although management remains optimistic about net interest income (NII) growth in 2025, Ostian warns that inflationary pressures, tariff concerns, and rising consumer credit risks could test that upbeat outlook. She advises a cautious stance until the earnings report provides updated clarity. 

On the other hand, SA contributor Paul Franke adopts a more bearish stance on Bank of America, assigning it a Sell rating. He warns that the bank's sizable exposure to consumer and small business loans leaves it particularly vulnerable amid growing macroeconomic pressures. Franke also flags Warren Buffett’s decision to trim his BAC holdings in 2024 as a concerning signal. Looking ahead, he cautions that a potential 2025 recession—possibly fueled by stagflationary policies and a pullback in Wall Street wealth, could significantly pressure the stock. Additionally, his technical momentum analysis, including weak readings from On-Balance Volume (OBV) and Ease of Movement (EMV), suggests the risk of further downside.

  • Consensus EPS Estimates: $0.81
  • Consensus Revenue Estimates: $26.91B
  • Earnings Insight: The bank has exceeded EPS expectations in 6 of the past 8 quarters, and revenue expectations in only one of those reports.

Also reporting: Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Citigroup (NYSE:C), United Airlines (UAL), Ericsson (ERIC), PNC Financial Services Group (PNC), Omnicom Group (OMC), Interactive Brokers Group (IBKR), J.B. Hunt Transport Services (JBHT), Albertsons Companies (ACI), and more.

Wednesday, April 16

Abbott Laboratories (NYSE:ABT)

Healthcare conglomerate Abbott Laboratories (NYSE:ABT) is scheduled to report its Q1 financial results before the market opens on Wednesday.

Recently, the company secured FDA approval for an investigational device exemption tied to its Coronary Intravascular Lithotripsy System, aimed at treating severe calcification in coronary arteries before stenting. The clinical trial is expected to enroll up to 335 participants across 47 sites in the U.S.-

Meanwhile, Abbott was recently named among the 30 most preferred stocks across all 11 S&P sectors by the UBS CIO Americas wealth management equity sector strategy team.

Abbott receives a Hold rating from Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating system, in contrast to a consensus Buy rating from Wall Street analysts.

SA contributor Motti Sapir takes a bullish stance on ABT, arguing that it is undervalued due to strong fundamentals, robust cash flow, and strategic moves in high-growth areas like diabetes and weight-loss care. The company’s FreeStyle Libre system and new Protality brand support its long-term growth trajectory. With a net income margin of nearly 32% and over $8.5B in operational cash flow, Sapir assigns a 12–18-month price target of $145–$160, calling Abbott a compelling opportunity for growth, stability, and income.

  • Consensus EPS Estimates: $1.07
  • Consensus Revenue Estimates: $10.41B
  • Earnings Insight: The company has exceeded EPS and revenue expectations in 7 of the past 8 quarters.

Also reporting: Kinder Morgan (NYSE:KMI), ASML Holding (ASML), Alcoa (AA), U.S. Bancorp (USB), CSX (CSX), Prologis (PLD), Travelers Companies (TRV), Citizens Financial Group (CFG), SL Green Realty (SLG), Bank OZK (OZK), Progressive (PGR), Martin Midstream Partners LP (MMLP), and more.

Thursday, April 17

Netflix (NASDAQ:NFLX)

Streaming giant Netflix (NASDAQ:NFLX) is set to report Q1 results on Thursday after the bell. Shares have climbed 49% over the past 12 months, but are up only 3% YTD.

The Seeking Alpha Quant Rating turned cautious in April, downgrading the stock to Hold from Strong Buy, even as Wall Street analysts maintain Buy ratings.

SA contributor YR Research remains bullish, citing Netflix’s transformation into a “Need” over a “Want” with over 300M subscribers, an unmatched content library, and a leading digital platform. The contributor’s proprietary data points to 8% viewership growth in Q1, alongside record-high Nielsen viewing share, supporting expectations of solid subscriber and revenue gains.

While acknowledging the stock’s premium valuation, YR Research believes consensus estimates are conservative, assigning a $1,000 price target based on a 33x multiple on projected 2026 earnings.

  • Consensus EPS Estimates: $5.74
  • Consensus Revenue Estimates: $10.51B
  • Earnings Insight: Netflix has exceeded EPS estimates in 7 of the past 8 quarters, while exceeding revenue expectations in just 6 of those reports.

Also reporting: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (NYSE:TSM), UnitedHealth Group (UNH), Blackstone (BX), American Express (AXP), Truist Financial Corporation (TFC), D.R. Horton (DHI), Charles Schwab (SCHW), Huntington Bancshares (HBAN), KeyCorp (KEY), Regions Financial (RF), Infosys Technologies (INFY), Ally Financial (ALLY), Fifth Third Bancorp (FITB), and more.

Friday, April 18

Good Friday, the U.S. markets will be closed.

data.title: Plunging oil prices force U.S. oil industry to consider cutting production, jobs
data.description:
Despite President Trump's exhortations to "drill, baby, drill," U.S. shale oil producers are facing their most serious crisis since the pandemic, as the sudden oil price selloff triggered in part by President Trump's trade war has pushed parts of the sector to the brink of failure, industry executives warned this week.

Oil markets have been hit with a potential "double whammy" of declining demand from a tariff-induced economic slowdown combined with new supplies, with OPEC+ announcing last week it planned to raise production faster than expected in the coming months.

Analysts say Trump's decision to leave tariffs on China, the world's biggest oil importer, will continue to loom over global crude demand prospects.

The U.S. Energy Information Administration this week sharply cut its estimate of U.S. crude prices to $63.88/bbl for 2025 from a prior forecast of $70.68/bb, and lowered its forecast for global oil consumption growth for this year by 400K bbl/day to 900K bbl/day.

Tariffs also will make it more expensive to buy steel and equipment, which could further discourage drilling unless oil prices rise substantially.

Benchmark U.S. crude has fallen nearly 14% so far this month, leaving the price below the level many producers in Texas say they need to break even and even causing concern that the industry could be forced to idle rigs.

Rystad Energy said this week that many U.S. shale producers faced breakeven costs of ~$62/bbl when debt servicing and dividend payments were included, and "in reality, even a company operating on $40 breakeven acreage would be inclined to slow down activity when prices fall below $65/bbl, as their level of dividend coverage would be at risk," Rystad said.

Energy prices remain well below levels producers say they need before they substantially increase activity and will not get there any time soon, according to the Federal Reserve Bank of Kansas City's latest quarterly survey, which said the energy firms surveyed need to see oil at $85/bbl and natural gas at $5.10/MMBtu before they significantly ramp up drilling.

"If oil does go into lower $60s or upper $50s, public independents that are already capital disciplined are going to have to cut their budget and cut rigs," said Bryan Sheffield, founder of energy investors Formentera Partners and former Parsely Energy CEO.

"If prices get sub-$60 and stay there, we'll see a definite drop in the rig count," Marauder Capital managing partner Roe Patterson said. "They've definitely opened the door for the OPEC countries to gain market share here, and it's an inadvertent, self-inflicted wound."

While it may cost under $40/bbl to drill in the best parts of the Permian Basin, new well drilling in North Dakota would require oil prices at ~$57/bbl, according to Wood Mackenzie, meaning operations in those basins would be more at risk at current price levels.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:XLE), (NYSEARCA:XOP), (AMLP), (VDE), (OIH), (IXC), (CRAK)
